Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Nonlinear Pricing Complexity
and Consumer Behavior
(Exploratory) evidence from a uniquely frustrating experiment
Joshua Deutschmann, Jeﬀrey D. Michler, Emilia Tjernstr¨om
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Motivation: ﬁrm perspective
Nonlinear pricing mechanisms can increase proﬁts over linear prices. Caveat...
Long menus of costs 2k −1 for k goods
Assumes perfect knowledge of demand space
Assumes consumers do not make mistakes
Real world examples
Cable TV providers (Crawford 2008)
Digital media (Shiller & Waldfogel 2011)
Tickets to Broadway plays (Leslie 2004)
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Motivation: behavioral perspective
Nonlinear mechanisms can beneﬁt consumers by oﬀering “tailored” choices.
Caveat...
People make mistakes
Assumes no search costs
Assumes no stress or cognitive costs
Real world examples
Value perception & bundling (Brough & Chernev 2012)
Gym memberships (DellaVigna & Malmendier 2006)
Bandwidth & deliberation Costs (Schilbach et al. 2016; Spears 2014))
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
The known unknown
Fundamental tension in nonlinear pricing:
More options may ↑consumer surplus but also ↑cognitive costs, ↑probability of
mistakes, ↑ﬁrm uncertainty regarding demand
No closed-form solutions
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
The known unknown
Fundamental tension in nonlinear pricing:
More options may ↑consumer surplus but also ↑cognitive costs, ↑probability of
mistakes, ↑ﬁrm uncertainty regarding demand

How do real people respond to complex nonlinear prices?
Reduce performance

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Research questions
1 How does price complexity aﬀect consumer choice & welfare?
2 How do price shocks / search diﬃculty interact with complexity in this context?
3 Do these eﬀects result in changes to optimal ﬁrm behavior?
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
What we do
Simulate optimal prices (following Chu et al. 2011; Eckalbar 2010)
Run a lab experiment to measure choice behavior & potential errors
how much do participants deviate from optimal?
do participants do worse with complex prices?
does complexity cause participants to drop out of the market?
Coming: update price simulation with participant behavior
how does participant behavior aﬀect proﬁts?
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
What we do & what we ﬁnd
Simulate optimal prices (following Chu et al. 2011; Eckalbar 2010)
Run a lab experiment to measure choice behavior & potential errors
how much do participants deviate from optimal? Quite a bit!
do participants do worse with complex prices? Yep.
does complexity cause participants to drop out of the market? Looks like it.
Coming: update price simulation with participant behavior
how does participant behavior aﬀect proﬁts? Don’t know yet!
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Generalized setup
Monopolist ﬁrm selling N goods
Consumer valuations: Vi = [Vi1, ..., ViN]
Each valuation drawn from uniform distribution
Unit demand for each good
Consumers maximize surplus: V ′
i Dj −pj
Dj K × 1 binary vector indicating goods in bundle j
pj price of bundle j

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Pricing strategies
Component Pricing (CP)
Set price for each good
No bundle discount oﬀered
k prices
Pure Bundling (PB)
Set price for bundle
No separate sale
1 price
Mixed Bundling (MB)
Set price for each good
Set price for each combination
2k −1 prices
Bundle Sized Pricing (BSP)
Set price for a single good
Set price for each bundle size
k prices
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Experimental design
Random reservation values for 6 goods
Constant for each participant throughout experiment
6 periods of 6 rounds each
In each round, randomly draw:
Pricing scheme (CP, PB, MB, BSP)
Number of goods (2-5)
Type of goods (color)
Prices simulated-optimal
Interface shows cost and surplus of current selection(s)
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Pre-analysis plan
Several clear primary hypotheses, which we pre-register
Many low-weight secondary hypotheses
It can cost more in power to pre-register a hypothesis if either of these are
suﬃciently small (Anderson & Magruder 2017):
the importance of the hypothesis, uh
the prior probability you assign to H0 being false, ph
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Split sample intuition (A&M, 2017)
Imagine a main hypothesis, with power 0.8 (in full data set)
Consider a 2nd hypothesis, same power but ph is only 0.1
If we correct for multiple hypothesis testing, then power on both is now 0.71
So we sacriﬁce 9% power on main hyp, and gained only 7% power on second
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
“Hybrid” pre-analysis plan
Follow “hybrid” split-sample strategy
pre-register main hypotheses, test them in full sample
use 35% exploratory sample (N=246) to try out secondary
update pre-registration with those secondary hypotheses that pass slightly more
lenient bar
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Regression speciﬁcation
Yit = α + β1Bit + β2Nit + β3(Bit × Nit) + β4Git + γi + λt + ϵit
Bit: complex price dummy
Nit: number of goods
Git: other round-level game characteristics
γi: participant ﬁxed eﬀects
λ: round FE

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
Boundedly rational consumers?
Consumers clearly make mistakes but what type of mistakes?
1 Not mistakes, failure to fully account for search costs
Consumers are rational; ﬁrms just needs to account for this
2 Overconﬁdent or biased mis-weighting of options
Leads to systematic distortions in demand that ﬁrms can exploit by distorting price
3 Fail to choose the best price due to sub-optimal search, confusion comparing
bundles, or excessive inertia
Leads to noise in demand that is uncorrelated across consumers and may be hard for
ﬁrms to fully adjust
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om

Introduction
Theoretical environment
Experimental design
Exploratory results
Conclusions
More work to do!
Characterize types of non-optimal behavior
Re-simulate optimal ﬁrm behavior based on revealed demand
in particular, do consumer errors change which is the proﬁt-maximizing mechanism?
Click behavior during games
Eﬀect of feedback between periods
Interaction between response time, performance, and drop-out
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior
Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernstr¨om
